nutrient_source.title: Carp, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 15017
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Carp, raw (NDB No. 15017 / FDC ID 171955), an approved secondary global source. The HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F03010010) was the original data source for this record and reports identical values for energy (127 kcal), protein (17.83 g), fat (5.6 g), carbohydrate (0 g), sodium (49 mg), potassium (333 mg), calcium (41 mg), iron (1.24 mg), and cholesterol (66 mg) per 100 g, consistent with USDA SR Legacy. USDA FoodData Central is cited as the primary approved source to satisfy G2 compliance. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is taken from USDA SR Legacy for carp, raw.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Carp, raw, NDB No. 15017)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable; carbohydrate content is 0 g per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
gi_source.source_match_notes: Carp, raw contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15017). Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition (GL = GI x carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = not applicable). gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (49 mg/100 g) with high potassium (333 mg/100 g), yielding a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Raw carp is suitable for individuals with hypertension; prepare by steaming, baking, or poaching without added salt, and avoid high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or salted marinades to preserve its naturally low sodium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (17.83 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw carp has no glycaemic impact and is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid sweet glazes, sugar-based marinades, or high-GI accompaniments to maintain blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.6 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g), presence of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), and low sodium support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw carp is a heart-healthy protein source; choose steaming or baking over deep-frying to avoid adding saturated or trans fats, and limit sodium-rich sauces to maintain the food's favourable cardiovascular profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Presence of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties; low saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Carp provides omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for reducing joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; prefer steaming or baking over deep-frying to avoid generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that can worsen joint inflammation, and consider supplementing with higher-omega-3 marine fish such as salmon or sardines for greater anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carp (Cyprinus carpio) is a large freshwater fish widely consumed across Asia, Europe, and North America. In its raw state, carp has silvery-grey scales and pink-white flesh with a mild, slightly earthy flavour and firm texture. It is a versatile culinary ingredient used in steamed, braised, fried, and soup preparations across Chinese, Eastern European, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Nutritionally, raw carp provides 127 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 17.83 g, moderate fat of 5.6 g (predominantly unsaturated, with only 1.1 g saturated fat), and zero carbohydrate and sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is very low at 49 mg/100 g, while potassium is high at 333 mg/100 g, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Carp also provides 41 mg calcium, 1.24 mg iron, and 66 mg cholesterol per 100 g. Its omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA/DHA) contributes anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Glycemic index is not applicable as the food contains negligible carbohydrates. Carp is halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is a common protein source in Singapore, China, Malaysia, and Eastern Europe.
